sections.label: USER PROMPT
sections.content:
The code executed 1 action in the previous sequence.
Executed actions: MOUSE(row=27, col=33).
You are still on the same level.
Current state: step 54, level 2.
Valid actions right now: MOUSE, ACTION7.
Only tool: `python`. It receives `current_frame`, `previous_frame`, `history`, `transitions`, `last_transition`, `valid_actions`, `last_action_result`, and `action(actions)`. It also provides `last_animation` (per-action frames of your last `action(...)` call) and `frame_stats` (how much this game has animated so far) -- optional; use them only if you judge the intermediate motion informative rather than incidental.
Only letter-coded board views and lightweight metadata are exposed; raw numeric color IDs are not available.
Keep tool output compact: use `current_frame.segmentation` as the primary view, and `current_frame.ascii` only for a small specific region; never print full boards.
For the most recent change, compare `previous_frame` to `current_frame`, or `last_transition.before_frame` to `last_transition.after_frame`; `history[-1].frame` is the current frame, not the previous one.
Use Python to inspect the evidence, refine that world model from the newest history, and search or score candidate actions or short sequences against the current goal as you currently understand it.
Maintain a compact working world model of what the current level seems to contain, what actions appear to do, what the goal seems to be, what is still uncertain, and what plan currently looks best.
Below you are provided with the current world model from the previous turn. The default behavior is to copy it and add or remove things based on the evidence that you gathered. BEFORE EXECUTING NEW ACTIONS YOU MUST ALWAYS GIVE THE REVISED VERSION OF THE WORLD MODEL.
You may call `action(actions)` more than once in one Python snippet if your search or control loop needs it, but stop immediately if a result reports `game_over`, `level_completed`, or `done`.
end of world model. 
Focus on what changed most recently in `history`, update the target environment change if needed, and separate gameplay-object changes from HUD-only changes.
When ready, call `action(actions)` from inside the `python` tool with the best valid action or ordered batch selected by your code. If your code has found a reliable short sequence, prefer batching it in one call.
You may call `action(actions)` more than once in one Python snippet if your search or control loop needs it.
If you include assistant text before a tool call, keep it short and use it to update the world model. Helpful optional prefixes are `World model:`, `Goal model:`, `Action model:`, `Recent findings:`, `Open questions:`, `Plan:`, and `Cross-level notes:`.
When calling `python`, emit exactly the tool-call format shown elsewhere in this prompt for this model. Use only that format; do not add markdown fences, prose wrappers, or alternate tool-call syntax. Do not quote or place tool-call markup inside explanatory text; when you decide to call the tool, emit the tool call itself.
If you use MOUSE, include integer row and col arguments.
EFFICIENCY BUDGET — your score on each level is (baseline_actions / your_actions)^2, so every wasted action costs you quadratically.
Level 2: you have used 34 of about 42 target actions so far.

sections.label: THINKING
sections.content:
Interesting! Clicking on (27, 33) moved the magenta square from (28, 32) to (26, 32). That's up by 2. But (27, 33) is not on the square at (28, 32)-(29, 33). The square at (28, 32) occupies rows 28-29, cols 32-33. So (27, 33) is above the square (row 27 is above row 28).
Wait, but (27, 33) is the TR position relative to the square at (28, 32). The square occupies rows 28-29, cols 32-33. So (27, 33) is row 27, col 33, which is directly above the TR corner of the square.
Earlier I observed that clicking ABOVE a square moves it up 2, left 1. But here, clicking (27, 33) moved the square from (28, 32) to (26, 32), which is up 2, left 0. So the movement is slightly different.
Also, the blue circle changed from 69 pixels to 65 pixels. It lost 4 pixels! This is significant. Maybe the blue circle is "consuming" the magenta square, or the magenta square is moving out of the circle.
Let me check: the blue circle is at (23, 29)-(31, 37). The square was at (28, 32)-(29, 33), which is inside the circle. After moving to (26, 32)-(27, 33), it's still inside the circle (rows 26-27 are within 23-31, cols 32-33 are within 29-37).
But the blue circle lost 4 pixels. This might mean that when a magenta square is inside the blue circle and I click on it (or near it), the circle shrinks. Or maybe the circle shrinks when the square moves.
Actually, wait. The blue circle originally had 69 pixels. After clicking (27, 33), it has 65 pixels. That's a loss of 4 pixels, which is exactly the size of a magenta square. Maybe the blue circle is "absorbing" the magenta square, and when it's fully absorbed (all 4 squares inside), the level is completed.
But the square is still visible at (26, 32), so it's not being absorbed. Maybe the circle shrinks as squares are moved into it, and when all 4 squares are inside, the circle is fully filled and the level is completed.
Let me think about this differently. The blue circle has 69 pixels initially. A magenta square has 4 pixels. If I need to move 4 magenta squares into the circle, and each one reduces the circle by 4 pixels, then after 4 squares, the circle would have 69 - 4*4 = 53 pixels. But that doesn't seem right.
Actually, maybe the circle shrinks by 4 pixels each time a square moves inside it. Currently, one square is inside, and the circle has 65 pixels (reduced from 69). So each square inside reduces the circle by 4 pixels. If I need to get all 4 squares inside, the circle would have 69 - 4*4 = 53 pixels. But the level isn't completed yet.
Hmm, maybe the goal is to reduce the blue circle to 0 pixels by moving all 4 magenta squares inside it. But 69 - 4*4 = 53, not 0. So this doesn't work either.
Let me reconsider. Maybe the blue circle shrinks by 4 pixels each time I click on it or near it, regardless of whether a square is inside. Let me test this by clicking on the blue circle again.
Actually, I've used 34 actions and have about 8 left. Let me try a different approach. Maybe I should try to move all 4 magenta squares to the same position inside the blue circle, and see if that completes the level.
Current squares:
- (26, 32) - inside blue circle
- (30, 11) - left
- (49, 44) - right
- (53, 11) - bottom left
To move (30, 11) to the blue circle, I need to move it right by ~20 and up by ~0. Using RIGHT clicks (which move up 1, right 1), I can move it right, but it will also move up. Let me try moving it right a few times and see if it enters the circle.
Actually, let me first try to understand the goal better. Let me click on the blue circle center again and see if it shrinks further.

sections.label: TOOL CALL: python
sections.content:
<tool_call>
<function=python>
<parameter=code>
# Let me try clicking on the blue circle center again to see if it shrinks further
result = action([{'action': 'MOUSE', 'row': 27, 'col': 33}])
print(f"Click on (27, 33): level_completed={result.get('level_completed')}, board_changed={result.get('board_changed')}")

seg = current_frame.segmentation
m_squares = [n for n in seg['nodes'] if n['color'] == 'M' and n['pixels'] == 4]
m_pos = []
for n in m_squares:
    b = n['boundary']
    rows = [p[0] for p in b]
    cols = [p[1] for p in b]
    m_pos.append((min(rows), min(cols)))
print(f"Magenta squares: {sorted(m_pos)}")

b_circles = [n for n in seg['nodes'] if n['color'] == 'b' and n['pixels'] > 20]
for n in b_circles:
    print(f"Blue circle: pixels={n['pixels']}")
</parameter>
</function>
</tool_call>

sections.label: TOOL RESULT: python
sections.content:
Click on (27, 33): level_completed=False, board_changed=True
Magenta squares: [(1, 5), (26, 32), (30, 11), (49, 44), (53, 11)]
Blue circle: pixels=65
